skill.name: azure-ai-translation-document-py
skill.description: Python guidance for Azure AI Document Translation. It shows batch and single-document translation, Azure authentication, status checks, glossaries, and supported formats.
skill.summary: Translate documents with Azure AI Document Translation using Python SDK examples.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command execution patterns, but they are Markdown code fences and shell examples, not executable skill code. The skill is legitimate Azure SDK documentation, but it handles API keys, SAS URLs, outbound Azure calls, and local documents. Publishing is acceptable with warnings about secret handling and avoiding logs that expose signed storage URLs.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Azure Credentials and SAS URLs in Examples
medium_findings.description: The examples read an Azure endpoint, API key, and source and target Blob Storage SAS URLs from environment variables. This is expected for Azure Document Translation, but leaked environment values can grant service or storage access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly access named credential and signed URL environment variables. The usage is legitimate, but the sensitive nature of the values is clear.

medium_findings.title: Potential SAS Token Disclosure in Status Logging
medium_findings.description: The status example prints each source document URL. If signed Blob URLs are returned, logs may expose SAS tokens or storage paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code prints document URLs, and this workflow uses SAS URLs for Blob Storage. Actual token exposure depends on Azure response details and logging context.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The repeated Ruby or shell backtick detections are Markdown code fences around examples. They do not execute commands by themselves and are false positives for direct command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious backticks are visible Markdown fences with bash or Python language labels. No executable script wrapper or runtime hook is present in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: Expected Outbound Azure Service URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are placeholders for Azure Cognitive Services and Blob Storage endpoints. They are required for document translation and do not indicate exfiltration to an unrelated domain.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs use Azure service host patterns and placeholder resource names. They match the documented purpose of the skill.

low_findings.title: Local Document Read and Write Example
low_findings.description: The single-document example reads a local DOCX file and writes the translated result. This is normal document processing, but users should choose output paths carefully.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations use fixed example filenames and support the translation workflow. No overwrite loop, hidden path, or unauthorized collection pattern is shown.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hit Is Not Supported by Evidence
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography finding points to the skill description. No cryptographic algorithm or hash function is shown at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain plain metadata text about Azure AI translation. I did not find evidence of weak cryptographic code there.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Static Hit Is a Status Identifier
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged operation status handling as reconnaissance. The code stores a translation operation identifier and lists document statuses for the same job.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines use a service operation ID from the Azure SDK. They do not inspect the host system or enumerate local resources.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential and SAS URL Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples depend on environment variables that can contain API keys and signed Blob Storage URLs. Users should keep these values out of prompts, logs, and shared transcripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The variable names explicitly identify Azure keys and storage container URLs. The pattern is sensitive even though it is expected for the service.

dangerous_patterns.title: Signed URL Logging Risk
dangerous_patterns.description: The status example prints source document URLs. Signed URLs should be redacted before logging or sharing output.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code prints a URL in a workflow that uses SAS-authenticated containers. The exact sensitivity depends on whether Azure returns signed URLs.

content.value_statement: Document translation projects need repeatable Python examples for Azure AI setup and job tracking. This skill provides focused SDK patterns for batch translation, single files, glossaries, and status checks.
actual_capabilities: Shows installation for the Azure AI Document Translation Python package.
Demonstrates API key and DefaultAzureCredential authentication patterns.
Provides batch translation examples for Azure Blob Storage containers.
Shows single-document translation with local file input and output.
Covers job status, document status, cancellation, glossaries, and supported formats.
Includes async client usage for concurrent translation workflows.
limitations: Requires an existing Azure AI Translator resource and Azure Blob Storage containers.
Does not create cloud resources, assign permissions, or generate SAS tokens.
Examples are documentation snippets, not a complete production application.
Users must protect API keys, credentials, and signed storage URLs.

use_cases.title: Batch Translate Business Documents
use_cases.description: Translate Word, PDF, Excel, and PowerPoint files from one storage container to another.

use_cases.title: Build Translation into a Python App
use_cases.description: Add Azure document translation calls, authentication, and polling to an existing Python service.

use_cases.title: Review Translation Job Health
use_cases.description: Check operation status, document outcomes, failures, supported languages, and supported file formats.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up Translation
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for environment variables and authentication.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to configure Azure AI Document Translation in Python using environment variables and DefaultAzureCredential.

prompt_templates.title: Translate a Batch
prompt_templates.scenario: Basic batch translation from one container to another.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Python example that translates documents from my source container to Spanish in a target container.

prompt_templates.title: Track Job Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me list translation operations, inspect each document status, and summarize failed documents without printing signed URLs.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Production Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced architecture for secure, concurrent translation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a secure async Python workflow for translating documents into three languages with separate containers and glossary support.

output_examples.input: Translate all documents in an Azure Blob container to Spanish.
output_examples.output: A Python workflow that authenticates to Azure, starts a batch translation job, waits for completion, and reports success counts.

output_examples.input: Check which documents failed in a translation operation.
output_examples.output: A status review approach that lists document outcomes, captures error messages, and avoids exposing signed source URLs.

output_examples.input: Translate one DOCX file from local storage.
output_examples.output: A single-document translation pattern that reads the file, sends it to Azure, and writes the translated document locally.

best_practices: Use minimal SAS permissions for source and target containers.
Prefer DefaultAzureCredential when your environment supports managed identity.
Redact API keys, SAS tokens, and document URLs before logging output.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real API keys or signed URLs into prompts or shared logs.
Do not use one target container for every language when outputs must stay separated.
Do not submit unsupported formats without checking supported document formats first.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you use the Azure AI Document Translation Python SDK for batch and single-document translation.

faq.question: Does it translate documents without Azure?
faq.answer: No. You need an Azure AI Translator resource and the required storage access.

faq.question: Which document formats are covered?
faq.answer: The guide lists common formats such as DOCX, PDF, PPTX, XLSX, HTML, TXT, CSV, JSON, XML, and XLIFF.

faq.question: Can I use managed identity?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes a DefaultAzureCredential example for Entra ID authentication.

faq.question: Does it include glossary support?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how to attach a glossary file from Azure Blob Storage.

faq.question: What security precautions matter most?
faq.answer: Protect API keys and SAS URLs. Redact signed document URLs before storing or sharing logs.
